items.back: State the word Victor or the letter J followed by the airway or route number in group form.

items.front: How is a VOR, VORTAC, or TACAN alternate airway stated?
items.back: State Victor followed by the airway number in group form and the alternate direction.

items.front: How are named routes such as NARs and Bahama Routes stated?
items.back: State North American Route or Bahama Route followed by the route number in group form.

items.front: How are ATS routes like R20 or A50 stated?
items.back: State the route letters phonetically, followed by the route number in group form.

items.front: How are military training routes stated?
items.back: State the letters I-R or V-R followed by the route number in group form.

items.front: How are published RNAV routes stated?
items.back: High altitude routes use the letter Q followed by the route number in group form. Low altitude routes use the route letter phonetically followed by the route number in group form.

items.front: How are VOR airways and jet routes described?
items.back: VOR airways: 'Victor' followed by the number in group form. Jet routes: 'J' followed by the number in group form. Per 2-5-1(a).

items.front: When a NAVAID is used in routing, how may it be identified?
items.back: State the NAVAID name or the phonetic alphabet equivalent of its location identifier.

items.front: When a NAVAID is used as a clearance limit and the type is known, how is it stated?
items.back: State the NAVAID name followed by the type of NAVAID.

items.front: How are radials, azimuths, or bearings from a VOR, VORTAC, TACAN, or GPS waypoint described?
items.back: State the NAVAID or waypoint name followed by the separate digits of the radial, azimuth, or bearing and the word radial, azimuth, or bearing.

items.front: How is an arc about a VOR-DME, VORTAC, or TACAN described?
items.back: State the distance in miles, the words mile arc, the direction from the NAVAID, the word of, and the NAVAID name.

items.front: How is a quadrant within a radius of a NAVAID described?
items.back: State the quadrant direction, followed by the distance in miles from the NAVAID.

items.front: How is a nondirectional beacon reference described?
items.back: State the course to or bearing from the beacon, the beacon name, and the words radio beacon.

items.front: How is an MLS reference described?
items.back: State the azimuth to or from the MLS, the name of the MLS, and the term MLS.

items.front: How is an NRS waypoint stated?
items.back: State the ICAO FIR letter, the FIR subset letter, the latitude increment, and the longitude increment.

items.front: How are NAVAIDs described?
items.back: By stating the name or phonetic spelling followed by the type of NAVAID. Per 2-5-2(a).

items.front: How is an unnamed fix based on a radial, localizer, or azimuth and distance described?
items.back: State the NAVAID name, the specified radial, localizer, or azimuth, the distance in miles, and the phrase mile fix.

items.front: How is a fix stated when it is charted on a SID, STAR, en route chart, or approach plate?
items.back: State the name of the fix.

items.front: What expression should not be used to describe a fix?
items.back: Do not use expressions such as passing Victor Twelve or passing J Eleven.

items.front: What is the structure of the unnamed-fix example Appleton 050 radial 37-mile fix?
items.back: NAVAID name, specified radial, distance in miles, and the phrase mile fix.

items.front: How is an unnamed localizer-based fix stated in the example from 2-5-3?
items.back: Reno localizer back course Four mile fix.

items.front: When must controllers become familiar with pertinent weather information?
items.back: When coming on duty.

items.front: What weather awareness must continue after a controller comes on duty?
items.back: Stay aware of current and forecast weather information needed to perform ATC duties.

items.front: Why does 2-6-1 require continuing weather awareness?
items.back: Because weather information is needed to perform ATC duties safely and effectively.

items.front: What does the note under 2-6-1 say about weather and phases of flight?
items.back: Every phase of flight has the potential to be impacted by weather.

items.front: When must controllers familiarize themselves with weather?
items.back: When coming on duty, and stay aware of current and forecasted weather.

items.front: Which two PIREP categories receive special emphasis in 2-6-2?
items.back: Urgent (UUA) and routine (UA) PIREPs.

items.front: What ceiling threshold triggers PIREP solicitation under 2-6-2?
items.back: Ceilings at or below 5,000 feet.

items.front: What hourly PIREP requirement applies when approach control services are provided with ceilings at or below 5,000 feet?
items.back: Obtain at least one descent or climb-out PIREP each hour, plus related weather phenomena when available.

items.front: How is a low-level wind shear PIREP classified when the pilot reports airspeed fluctuations of 10 knots or more?
items.back: Urgent.

items.front: How is a low-level wind shear PIREP classified when reported airspeed fluctuations are less than 10 knots?
items.back: Routine.

items.front: How is a sulfur-gas-only cabin report classified when the pilot confirms no volcanic ash clouds are present?
items.back: Routine.

items.front: What extra details must be recorded with a turbulence PIREP?
items.back: Duration and intensity.

items.front: What extra details must be recorded with an icing PIREP?
items.back: Icing type and intensity, plus the air temperature in which the icing is occurring.

items.front: How are operationally significant PIREPs disseminated by en route facilities?
items.back: Relay them to the facility weather coordinator.

items.front: What types of PIREPs must be emphasized for solicitation and dissemination?
items.back: Urgent (UUA) and Routine (UA) PIREPs. Per 2-6-2.

items.front: When prevailing visibility is less than 4 miles, which value is used for aircraft operations if tower and surface observations differ?
items.back: Use the lower of the two observations.

items.front: When must tower visibility observations be forwarded to the weather observer?
items.back: When prevailing visibility is less than 4 miles and tower observations are being taken.

items.front: At what wind speed is the wind described as calm?
items.back: Less than three knots.

items.front: Which ceiling and visibility changes must be forwarded to the appropriate control facility?
items.back: Drops below the listed thresholds and improvements back above them.

items.front: What special weather reporting requirement applies at airports where military turbo-jet en route descents are routinely conducted?
items.back: Report the conditions to the ARTCC even if it is not the controlling facility.

items.front: What happens if the receiving facility says weather reports are not required for a specific time period?
items.back: Discontinue the reports for that period.

items.front: When must tower personnel take prevailing visibility observations?
items.back: When prevailing visibility at the usual point of observation or at tower level is less than 4 miles. Use the LOWER of tower and surface observations. Per 2-6-3(a)(1).

items.front: How may the area of weather or chaff coverage be defined when issuing information to aircraft?
items.back: By azimuth and distance from the aircraft, and/or by the width and coverage of the area in terms of fixes or distance and direction from fixes.

items.front: What term is used for satellite-derived weather?
items.back: Weather.

items.front: What term is used for radar-derived weather?
items.back: Precipitation.

items.front: What word should not be used when describing radar-derived weather?
items.back: Turbulence.

items.front: How do STARS precipitation levels correlate to intensity?
items.back: Level 1 light, Level 2 moderate, Levels 3 and 4 heavy, Levels 5 and 6 extreme.

items.front: How do en route controllers describe the lowest and highest displayable precipitation intensities?
items.back: The lowest displayable intensity is moderate, and the highest is heavy to extreme.

items.front: What must be issued when approving a weather deviation for an aircraft that had previously been issued a crossing altitude, including climb via or descend via?
items.back: Assign an altitude to maintain and, if necessary, a speed, and advise if the aircraft is expected to resume the procedure.

items.front: What does /F mean in en route fourth-line weather-deviation coordination?
items.back: The pilot has been authorized to deviate and rejoin the route of flight at the next NAVAID or waypoint in the flight plan.

items.front: What does the absence of a NAVAID, waypoint, or /F in the fourth line indicate?
items.back: It means the aircraft was approved to deviate for weather only; the receiving controller must clear it back on route, or verbal coordination is required if fourth-line text is insufficient.

items.front: What should the transferring controller coordinate when forwarding weather deviation information?
items.back: The nature of the route guidance service, including assigned headings, suggested headings, or pilot-initiated deviation.

items.front: What kind of weather information may be transmitted without consulting the weather reporting station?
items.back: General weather information without specific values, and elements derived directly from instruments, pilots, or radar.

items.front: What are the approved sources for transmitting specific ceiling and visibility values?
items.back: Use the official observer, a weather-station report, an FAA-approved automation surface weather system, or a properly certificated controller acting as the official observer.

items.front: May USAF controllers serve as official weather observers?
items.back: No.

items.front: What must be done when tower-observed weather elements differ from the weather station report?
items.back: Report the difference to the official observer for the element concerned.

items.front: Within what distance of the sector or area of jurisdiction must pilots be advised of hazardous weather that may impact operations?
items.back: Within 150 NM.

items.front: Which hazardous weather products are explicitly included in 2-6-6 advisories?
items.back: AIRMET outside the CONUS, SIGMET, Convective SIGMET, urgent PIREP, and Center Weather Advisory.

items.front: How must controllers disseminate hazardous inflight weather information on frequencies?
items.back: Broadcast it on all frequencies except the emergency frequency.

items.front: Where should pilots be sent if they request additional hazardous weather information?
items.back: The nearest Flight Service.

items.front: When may a tower cab or approach control facility limit hazardous weather broadcasts to events within 50 NM?
items.back: When any part of the described area is within 50 NM of the airspace under the facility's jurisdiction.

items.front: What must en route controllers do with hazardous weather information messages in ERAM after appropriate action has been taken?
items.back: Electronically acknowledge them.

items.front: Within what distance must controllers advise pilots of hazardous weather?
items.back: Within 150 NM of their sector or area of jurisdiction. Per 2-6-6.

items.front: Where must current altimeter settings be obtained from?
items.back: Direct-reading instruments or directly from weather reporting stations.

items.front: What should ATC do if a pilot requests the altimeter setting in millibars?
items.back: Ask the nearest weather reporting station for the equivalent millibar setting.

items.front: When is the term Estimated Altimeter used by USAF and USA controllers?
items.back: When the altimeter setting being reported or received is estimated.

items.front: What two direct sources are authorized for current altimeter settings under 2-7-1?
items.back: Direct-reading instruments and direct contact with weather reporting stations.

items.front: How must current altimeter settings be obtained?
items.back: From direct-reading instruments or directly from weather reporting stations. Per 2-7-1(a).

items.front: When must terminal controllers identify the source of an altimeter setting?
items.back: When the setting is for a location other than the aircraft's departure or destination airport.

items.front: When must en route controllers identify the source of an altimeter setting?
items.back: Whenever they issue an altimeter setting.

items.front: How is an altimeter setting issued if it is more than one hour old?
items.back: State the facility name, the altimeter setting, and the words more than one hour old.

items.front: How often must en route aircraft receive an altimeter setting while in your area of jurisdiction?
items.back: At least one time.

items.front: What is the general terminal rule for issuing altimeter settings to departures?
items.back: Issue the altimeter to all departures, except for approved local-operator and scheduled-air-carrier omissions.

items.front: When may the tower omit the altimeter for an arriving aircraft?
items.back: When approach control with jurisdiction has already sequenced or vectored the aircraft to the airport.

items.front: What extra altimeter-setting action is required for a nonprecision approach when the weather report says pressure falling rapidly?
items.back: Issue altimeter changes as frequently as practical in addition to the initial-contact altimeter.

items.front: What altimeter setting is used when clearing an aircraft below the lowest usable flight level?
items.back: The nearest weather reporting station to the point where the aircraft will descend below that flight level.

items.front: When do high barometric pressure procedures apply?
items.back: When the barometric pressure is greater than 31.00 inches mercury.

items.front: What are en route and arriving pilots told to do with the 31.00 setting during high barometric pressure procedures?
items.back: Leave the altimeter set to 31.00 until reaching the final approach fix.

items.front: What are departures told to do with the 31.00 setting during high barometric pressure procedures?
items.back: Set 31.00 prior to reaching any mandatory or crossing altitude or 1,500 feet AGL, whichever is lower.

items.front: What must affected ARTCCs do when pressure is above or forecast to be above 31.00 inches mercury?
items.back: Request a high barometric pressure NOTAM through the U.S. NOTAM Office.

items.front: Why can the cockpit altimeter and Mode C display differ during high barometric pressure procedures?
items.back: Because the transponder reports pressure altitude while ATC automation applies an altimeter setting to display altitude above MSL.

items.front: What happens when barometric pressure exceeds 31.00 inches Hg?
items.back: Issue the altimeter setting and advise high barometric pressure procedures are in effect. En Route/Arrivals: leave altimeter at 31.00 until reaching the final approach fix. Per 2-7-3.

items.front: When must RVR values be furnished for the runway in use?
items.back: Whenever the RVR equipment serving the runway in use is operational.

items.front: What later equipment status does not cancel the requirement to furnish RVR values under 2-8-1?
items.back: A later operation or nonoperation of navigational or visual aids used in applying RVR as takeoff or landing minima.

items.front: What is the minimum readout value of older RVR equipment?
items.back: 600 feet.

items.front: How low may newer RVR equipment read?
items.back: As low as 100 feet.

items.front: How may readout increments differ between older and newer RVR equipment?
items.back: Older equipment may use 200-foot increments, while newer equipment may use 100-foot increments below 800 feet.

items.front: When must RVR values be furnished?
items.back: Where RVR equipment is operational, for the runway in use — irrespective of the operational status of navigational or visual aids. Per 2-8-1.

items.front: When is touchdown RVR issued regardless of the prevailing visibility value?
items.back: When RVR indicates a reportable value.

items.front: What prevailing-visibility threshold requires touchdown RVR to be issued regardless of the value indicated?
items.back: 1 mile or less.

items.front: What is a reportable RVR value under 2-8-2?
items.back: RVR 6,000 feet or less.

items.front: When is RVR data not acceptable for reporting because of a difference from actual conditions?
items.back: When a reliable source determines it differs by more than 400 feet within the transmissometer area.

items.front: Which sources are considered reliable for determining that RVR data is not representative?
items.back: A certified weather observer, AWOS, air traffic controller, flight service specialist, or pilot.

items.front: When must midpoint and rollout RVR be issued along with touchdown RVR?
items.back: When either midpoint or rollout is below 2,000 feet and touchdown RVR is greater than that value.

items.front: Who must issue current RVR to each aircraft prior to landing or departure as required?
items.back: Local control.

items.front: When must touchdown RVR be issued?
items.back:
1. Visibility ≤1 mile
2. RVR indicates reportable value

items.front: What is the base structure of an RVR report?
items.back: State the runway, the abbreviation RVR, and the indicated value.

items.front: How are multiple RVR systems on the runway in use reported?
items.back: In terms of touchdown, mid, and rollout as appropriate.

items.front: How are off-scale RVR values reported?
items.back: Use more than or less than the appropriate minimum or maximum readable value.

items.front: How is rapidly varying RVR reported when the variance is 1,000 feet or more?
items.back: State the current value followed by the range of visibility variance.

items.front: Where does RVR appear when issued along with other weather elements?
items.back: In the normal sequence used for weather reporting.

items.front: What is the standard format for providing RVR information?
items.back: State the runway, the abbreviation RVR, and the indicated value. When with other weather elements, transmit in normal weather sequence. Per 2-8-3(a).

items.front: What is the purpose of ATIS under 2-9-1?
items.back: To provide advance noncontrol airport, terminal-area, and meteorological information to aircraft.

items.front: How is each ATIS message identified?
items.back: By a phonetic code word at both the beginning and the end of the message.

items.front: How are ATIS code words sequenced over time?
items.back: Sequentially from Alpha through Zulu, then repeated without regard to the beginning of a new day.

items.front: How is the first resumed ATIS message identified after an interruption of more than 12 hours?
items.back: With Alpha or the first assigned alphabet letter word.

items.front: What must be checked before an ATIS recording is transmitted?
items.back: Completeness, accuracy, speech rate, and proper enunciation.

items.front: What may separate arrival and departure ATIS broadcasts contain?
items.back: Only the information appropriate for that operation.

items.front: How are ATIS messages identified?
items.back: By a phonetic letter code at both the beginning and end.

items.front: When is a new ATIS recording made because of weather?
items.back: Whenever any new official weather is received, even if the values do not change.

items.front: What braking-action change requires a new ATIS recording?
items.back: When braking action reports indicate braking is worse than what the current ATIS includes.

items.front: What other kinds of changes can trigger a new ATIS recording under 2-9-2?
items.back: Any other pertinent change, such as runway change, approach in use, or new or canceled NOTAM or PIREP information.

items.front: After a pilot acknowledges the ATIS, when may controllers omit items already contained in it?
items.back: When those items are current; rapidly changing conditions are still issued by ATC.

items.front: What should ATC do when a pilot does not state the appropriate ATIS code on initial contact?
items.back: Ask the pilot to confirm receipt of the current ATIS information.

items.front: What must be issued if a pilot cannot receive the ATIS?
items.back: The current weather, runway in use, approach or departure information, pertinent NOTAMs, and airport conditions.

items.front: What must be included when broadcasting an ATIS code change on frequency?
items.back: The change in code and the known pertinent operational information that caused the change.

items.front: When must a new ATIS recording be made?
items.back: Upon receipt of ANY new official weather — regardless of whether values changed. Also when runway braking action reports change. Per 2-9-2(a)(1-2).

items.front: What core items belong in an ATIS broadcast as appropriate?
items.back: Facility name, code letter, latest weather time, weather and altimeter, and the approach and runway in use.

items.front: Which weather observation remarks must always be included in the ATIS when present?
items.back: Lightning, cumulonimbus, and towering cumulus clouds.

items.front: How long must a reported unauthorized laser illumination event remain on the ATIS?
items.back: For one hour following the last report.

items.front: Under what condition may ceiling or sky condition, visibility, and obstructions to vision be omitted from the ATIS?
items.back: When the ceiling is above 5,000 feet and the visibility is more than 5 miles.

items.front: What warning phraseology is required when a runway length has been shortened?
items.back: Warning must preface the runway number, shortened must be included, and the available length in feet must be stated.

items.front: What statement is required when a FICON NOTAM shows runway condition codes X/X/X?
items.back: Runway Condition Codes Missing.

items.front: What statement is added when runway condition codes are 3/3/3 and a Slippery When Wet FICON NOTAM applies?
items.back: Slippery When Wet.

items.front: What must the ATIS tell pilots to do on initial contact regarding the broadcast?
items.back: Acknowledge receipt of the ATIS message on initial contact.

items.front: What information is included in an ATIS broadcast?
items.back: Airport/facility name, phonetic letter code, time of latest weather (UTC), and weather information including wind, visibility, ceiling, temperature, dew point, and altimeter. Per 2-9-3(a).
